text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the pedicle of a large subserous fibroid, which appears calcified. The fibroid also has a small intramural component that is being dissected open.
The surgeon performs dissection to release the intramural and subserous fibroids, as identified on the MRI, from the anterior and posterior aspects of the uterus.
The surgeon identifies severe endometriosis along the tubal margins and the surface of the ovaries, performing ovarial lysis by releasing the endometriotic adhesions.
The surgeon has released the deep endometriosis, revealing the previously obliterated pouch of Douglas. Bilateral endometriosis is visible alongside multiple fibroids in the uterus.

The surgeon performs gentle vasopressin infiltration with approximately seven units in one hundred milliliters of normal saline. Following this, the dissection continues to release the posterior submucous, subserous, and intramural fibroids. Three fibroids are visibly released from the posterior aspect of the uterus.
The surgeon uses gentle bipolar current to control bleeding and maintain the plane of dissection while separating fibroids from the myoma bed, ensuring minimal damage to the uterine musculature.

The surgeon sutures the myoma beds on both the anterior and posterior aspects of the uterus using a quill suture in an interlocking pattern, ensuring the cavity is obliterated to prevent hematoma formation.
The surgeon places the fibroids inside a specialized containment bag designed for safe morcellation, preventing spillage of fibroid tissue into the peritoneal cavity during the procedure.

The surgeon introduces the morcellator into the specialized bag containing the fibroid specimens through one of its lateral ends. The fibroids are gently morcellated within the bag to prevent spillage into the peritoneal cavity. The specimens are then prepared for histopathological examination. During laparoscopy, the head of the operating table may be adjusted to prevent fibroids from slipping into the paracolic gutters due to their weight.
After completing the morcellation of fibroids, the surgeon proceeds to close the laparoscopic ports, marking the end of the surgical procedure.
